required_texts.passage: The LORD God said, 'It is not good for the man to be alone. I will make a helper suitable for him.' [...] So the LORD God caused the man to fall into a deep sleep; and while he was sleeping, he took one of the man's ribs and then closed up the place with flesh. Then the LORD God made a woman from the rib he had taken out of the man, and he brought her to the man. The man said, 'This is now bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh; she shall be called woman, for she was taken out of man.' (NIV)
required_texts.core_dispute: Does the ordering of creation establish a hierarchy of authority? What does ezer actually establish — and what does it not establish? How does the ANE context affect the argument that sequence implies hierarchy? What does this text establish for the church?

required_texts.passage: A woman should learn in quietness and full submission. I do not permit a woman to teach or to assume authority over a man; she must be quiet. For Adam was formed first, then Eve; and Adam was not the one deceived; it was the woman who was deceived and became a sinner. (NIV)
required_texts.core_dispute: What does authenteo mean and why does the decision matter? Does Paul's appeal to creation in verse 13 make the prohibition transcultural, and is that hermeneutical principle applied consistently elsewhere? How does the Ephesian context affect the reading? What is each discipline's honest verdict on what this text establishes?

required_texts.passage: But I want you to realize that the head of every man is Christ, and the head of the woman is man, and the head of Christ is God. Every man who prays or prophesies with his head covered dishonors his head. But every woman who prays or prophesies with her head uncovered dishonors her head [...] (NIV)
required_texts.core_dispute: Paul assumes women pray and prophesy in the gathered assembly. What does this establish about their roles — and why does Alderwood not build more on it? What is the head-covering argument actually doing? What does kephale mean — source or authority — and what follows from that decision?

required_texts.passage: As in all the congregations of the Lord's people. Women should remain silent in the churches. They are not allowed to speak, but must be in submission, as the law says. If they want to inquire about something, they should ask their own husbands at home; for it is disgraceful for a woman to speak in the church. (NIV)
required_texts.core_dispute: Is this a Corinthian slogan that Paul refutes in verse 36 (Peppiatt), a restriction on disruptive questioning, or a Pauline universal command? How does verse 36 function rhetorically? How do we hold this text alongside 1 Corinthians 11:5? What hermeneutical principle governs the holding?

required_texts.passage: There is neither Jew nor Gentile, neither slave nor free, nor is there male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus. (NIV)
required_texts.core_dispute: Is this soteriological only, or does it also carry ecclesiological weight? Does 'no male and female' quote Genesis 1:27, and what follows from that quotation? How much weight should this text carry for church structure, and what determines the answer?

required_texts.passage: I commend to you our sister Phoebe, a deacon of the church in Cenchreae [...] Greet Priscilla and Aquila, my co-workers in Christ Jesus [...] Greet Andronicus and Junia, my fellow Jews who have been in prison with me. They are outstanding among the apostles, and they were in Christ before I was. (NIV)
required_texts.core_dispute: Phoebe as diakonos and prostatis, Junia as en tois apostolois, Priscilla named before Aquila. What do these designations establish? What is the burden of proof for reading them restrictively rather than expansively? What does the history of interpretation of Junia's name and gender reveal?

required_texts.passage: Now Deborah, a prophet, the wife of Lappidoth, was leading Israel at that time. She held court under the Palm of Deborah between Ramah and Bethel in the hill country of Ephraim, and the Israelites went up to her to have their disputes decided. (Judges 4:4-5, NIV) // Hilkiah the priest, Ahikam, Akbor, Shaphan and Asaiah went to speak to the prophet Huldah, who was the wife of Shallum [...] She said to them, 'This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: Tell the man who sent you to me...' (2 Kings 22:14-15, NIV)
required_texts.core_dispute: What kind of authority do these women actually exercise? What does their presence in the canon establish — precedent, exception, or something that resists that binary? How do we decide, and what is each discipline's answer?
